speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Laura Singal. Today's date is the 27th of October, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Helen Granek. The interview is taking place in Melbourne, Victoria, Australia. Could you please state your full name and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Helen Granek, H-E-L-E-N G-R-A-N-E-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please state your full name at birth and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Helen Zylberblat, Z-Y-L-B-E-R-B-L-A-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Halfway through the war, which I'll tell you about later, my name was changed to Treyvish, T-R-E-Y-V-I-S-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: 10th of March, 1934.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your age?

speaker: interviewer
text: In what city were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe your family for me, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was an only child. My parents, what I remember of, were very, very loving. My father was chief accountant in a big steelworks firm. He was very, very well respected. He was of the utmost integrity, very loving, very warm with a fantastic sense of humor. And he and I had a wonderful relationship. My mother was very, very strong willed, very determined. I think if she lived in our times now, she would be a very big worker for human rights. She felt very deeply for people who were underprivileged. And felt-- and she hated injustice. She was very, very determined, a very good speaker, had a lovely singing voice, and was a wonderful mother from what I can remember. We lived in the main street in Warsaw, called Marszalkowska Street, on the first floor. We had quite a modern apartment. I had a lovely room. I still remember the layout of the furniture. My parents, their bedroom was like a bed sitting room. And there was like lounge furniture and a dining room table. And one room-- in one-- in the second bedroom, we always had a boarder. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had-- I have vivid recollection of my parents taking me out every Sunday to museums, to theaters. My parents were very well educated people. And they instilled in me a love of music, and art and humanity.

speaker: interviewer
text: What languages did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish. Up till-- up till the beginning of the war, I only spoke Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not very. They both came from traditional homes. They were not terribly observant, but I think they felt very strongly being Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you keep kosher?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. We had extended family in Bialystok. And although there were just the three of us living in Warsaw, there were visits to and from Warsaw that was my family on my father's side. My mother had an older brother who lived in a town called Vilna. And my mother's father, my grandfather, also lived there. My uncle had one son. He was a prominent member of the Vilna Jewish community. And every year, we spent about four or five weeks just outside Vilna as a family unit, holidaying. And that's quite significant, because we were there on the 1st of September, the day the war broke out. And we-- I remember hearing it on the radio, an emergency announcement and my mother packing very quickly. I was only five years old. I couldn't quite grasp the severity, the, the importance of the announcement. But after that, we, we must have gone by train from Vilna back to Warsaw, because my father wasn't with us. And we-- my father then came back from his holiday where he was into Warsaw. And there was the-- German was already-- Germany was already occupying Warsaw, so we, we made arrangements to leave Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just before we go on to leaving Warsaw, could you tell me a bit more about living in Warsaw before the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't really remember very, very much, whether there was-- I'm sure there was great anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you or your family ever encounter anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't remember because as I say, I hadn't even started kindergarten. So I was probably in a very sort of very small circle of-- I had some girl little friends because I remember dressing up and going to parties. But they were probably all Jewish, so I don't remember being exposed to the outside world.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your nicest memories from this period?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I said, going-- being-- dressing up. I loved dressing up and going to parties. And the Sundays when I went out either with just my father or both my parents. And just the entertainment that they provided for me was just lovely.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do when you went to Vilna, you were saying before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we went on holidays. We went to a-- it was like a huge forest. And there must  have been like a guest house. And that's where my mother and I stayed, and my late grandfather, and my uncle and my aunt and their son.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about that side of the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I said, my uncle was a very prominent member of the community. And the only thing I know of what happened to him is he and his son joined the partisans sometime during the war. And they were never heard of anymore. So we presumed after the war that they didn't survive-- we actually made inquiries-- that they didn't survive. My late uncles name was David. And because my whole-- my mother's whole family was wiped out, when my first son was born, I named him Michael after my father, and David after my late uncle. So at least the name is perpetuated.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your grandfather's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything about your grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just that I once asked him to look after my dolls.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did he?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm. I was running off to play. And I had a, a whole lot of dolls. And I asked him to look after. And I think he must have had quite a funny expression on his face. And he looked after my dolls. So I, I think I've got warm memories of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned before that your mother was a singer. Did she used to sing--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she wasn't a singer, she just had a lovely singing voice. And when there were gatherings or parties, she, she used to be asked-- she was asked to sing. And I also know that she was an excellent public speaker. She spoke beautifully. And I think she was called upon at gatherings or parties to say a few words. My mother was very strong. My father, I think, was a little bit weaker, but very, very loving, very lovable person. I remember once I was very naughty. And he punished me in some way. And then, I could see he was sitting in a chair terribly, terribly upset about it. And then he took me on his knee and he kissed me, and we made up. So he was very understanding and very warm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not before the war. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you were five years old when the war broke out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Five and a half, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were saying before, you were in Vilna. And then you came--

speaker: interviewee
text: We came back to Warsaw. And my parents realized that once the Germans occupied Warsaw, the Jewish people were in danger. So they thought that maybe if-- at, at this stage, the German's signed a pact with Russia where they divided Poland. Warsaw was under German occupation, and Bialystok was under Russian occupation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we ran away from Warsaw. We took our belongings, whatever we could. And it took a long time. We sort of stayed in the countryside. I don't even remember the mode of transport, but I think it must have been a horse and cart type of a transport. And we eventually came to Bialystok. Do you know how long it took you to get to Bialystok?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it seemed like weeks, but I can't really remember that well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was just the three of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a group of us. We weren't traveling just the three of us. There was a group. Whether they were my parent's friends or just people that they met up on the way, but there was a group of us traveling.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what sort of things did you take with you to Bialystok?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, probably just, just clothing and personal belongings. Nothing-- no, no, no furniture or anything. We just left the whole flat with the furniture, with everything. And I don't know what it-- because I've never been back to Warsaw, so I don't know what happened to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was life different for you in Bialystok, in comparison to Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, we lived with the extended family. I had an aunt and an uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Luba and Eugene Treyvish, and their daughter, Hanecka Treyvish. And my paternal grandmother lived with us also. So it was a-- the flat that we lived in had about five rooms. And my uncle was a doctor, so in the biggest room in the flat was the surgery. Then there was a waiting room. And the other three rooms we lived in. So there were three-- there were seven of us living in this place. Once the Russians came, my father got a job as an accountant. I went to a Russian kindergarten. My uncle worked as a-- he was a-- he was a surgeon, general surgeon and an orthopedic surgeon. So he worked in the main hospital in Bialystok. And life just went on. We didn't suffer terribly much. The Russians thought, I think, that they were going to occupy Bialystok for a long, long time. They were quite settled there, till June 1941, when the Germans broke the pact and invaded Russia, which meant that Bialystok was also invaded.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we go on to that, could you tell me a bit about going to kindergarten in Bialystok?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was actually a Russian kindergarten, so I-- there was-- I learned a little bit of reading and writing. And I learned Russian, because everything was conducted in Russian. So that was my first introduction to another language. And because my grandmother lived with us and she spoke mainly Yiddish, it was at that time that I started to pick up bits and pieces of Yiddish as well. And later on, when I was in the ghetto, in the camps, I learned Yiddish more fluently. So it was an introduction to languages. And I had friends. And I suppose I was just a normal school girl. My cousin was seven years older than I. So I remember some of her friends coming, and they played with me. And the flat was beautiful. It had a huge balcony. The rooms were quite big. And although we didn't have our own home anymore, it was a-- it was a fairly harmonious sort of household.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any anti-Semitic incidents during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Not during that time. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Was the ghetto in Bialystok established?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not then. Not till the Germans invaded Bialystok.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about the Germans invading in June 1941?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had been sent to Russia from his firm. And when he came-- there were-- there were rumors of impending war. And my father came back, I remember, on a Saturday. And everybody in Bialystok-- everybody, his family and his friends-- wanted to know what the mood was like in Russia. And he said, things are going on as normal. There's no sign of a German invasion. Everything is all right. And there were lots and lots of troop movements. And we could see there was something going on, but we couldn't really realize what it was. And then, overnight, from that Saturday night to Sunday, we heard airplanes coming across Bialystok and bombing. And it took the population of Bialystok a little while to realize that the Germans had actually invaded. And Bialystok was one of the first cities to be invaded once Germany declared war on Russia. And immediately, there were a lot of Jewish men who were rounded up in the street and transported and never seen again. One of the-- one of the main synagogues in Bialystok was set on fire.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that called?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Probably just a general-- a central synagogue or whatever. It was just the synagogue. And although I didn't fear the Germans because I was young and I didn't understand, I can now understand my parent's and my family's feelings. They knew that things would change for the worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear them talking about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes. They were quite heated discussions. My family were all very politically minded. And they all had different ideas about the world, about politics. And they used to sit up for hours in the evenings when they all came back from work and after dinner, and they had these very, very heated political discussions. And because I slept in that room where they-- in the lounge dining room where these discussions were taking place, I heard them. I can't say that I understood them, but I remember hearing heated discussions. Not-- people didn't fight, they just-- it was a healthy sort of argument.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember what they were talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, just whether the Russians would go, whether the Russians would stay, whether the Germans would stay. But they all knew that once the Germans came things would not be good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they make any plans?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I don't think so because, as I said, the invasion came very, very quickly. And although Bialystok was in Poland, it wasn't far from the Russian border. And parts of Russia were, were invaded overnight. So there was actually nowhere for my family to go. The rest of Poland had been under German domination already for a couple of years. Russia wasn't save-- safe. My mother actually thought that she might go and then get us over as well. But she didn't get very far, and she came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did she go?

speaker: interviewee
text: She tried to get across to Russia, but she couldn't. And my late uncle's sister was visiting us from Moscow, because she lived in Moscow at the time. And she straightaway got on a train thinking that she'd join her husband and her family in Moscow. But I believe she never got there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably-- she was probably killed on the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your mother leaving, trying to go to Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do. And then she came back very quickly because she had friends in Russia. And she thought if she could join them, then she would get us across as well. But it just didn't work out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you discuss this with your cousin?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. No. No, I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember feeling at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, probably fear. I could see the adults were very disturbed. And what had been a very happy household family, you know, everybody started-- everybody was nervous and upset. And there was a sense of doom.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that there was bombing as well when the Germans invaded. Where was this bombing?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bialystok.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember seeing anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, I remember there were places-- there were houses in ruin. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you describe the German invasion for me in June 1941?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Germans just marched into Bialystok. And because we lived in the main street, we could see through the front windows Germans marching in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did your parents react to this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think they were all very upset, very upset. And it wasn't long before the whole Jewish population were told to leave their homes and to go to a place, which they planned to convert into a ghetto, a part of Bialystok. We must have been given a little bit of notice because we were able to take furniture and household things and bedding and clothing and personal affects into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you remember packing up all this stuff and moving?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the space of time between the Germans marching in and--

speaker: interviewee
text: That, I don't know. I've often wondered about it, but I've just no way of knowing, because there aren't too many ghetto survivors from Bialystok still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember going to school during this time at all, before going into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I don't. Well, it was June, July-- no there, there-- I wasn't going to school, no. Maybe I still went to-- no, the Russian kindergarten would have been disbanded. So that was that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Germans established a ghetto in Bialystok.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. And my uncle, as I said, was a doctor. So for a while, he worked in a hospital outside the ghetto. And then there was a Jewish hospital established in the ghetto, and he was a surgeon there. He worked there till, till we left Bialystok in August 1943. My father also had a job-- there was sort of like, like a-- like a government, a Jewish government, like a-- yeah, like a small government.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Judenrate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Judenrate, yes, in the ghetto. And my father was an official there. He worked there. I don't think the women worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do as an official?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he would have worked as an accountant there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how he was chosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I just know that the place where we lived in the ghetto was quite a big apartment. And there were about five, six rooms. And in every room, there was a family living. So it was-- and every-- and we all shared the kitchen and the cooking. So it was quite cramped. But I think that I was taught privately in the ghetto. By privately, I mean there was no established school. There was just a few children would be put together, and a teacher would come and teach us. Because at some stage or another, I learned to read, and to write, and to count. So it must have been in the ghetto, because at that stage, I was already seven years old. So I would have started my schooling in the ghetto. But there was no formal schooling as such. I didn't go to school till after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was organized informally by your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Probably by several mothers. And they got a teacher in the ghetto. Maybe they paid her. Probably, they did. And, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and that's how we spent our time. I had some friends in the flats. And that's how we lived. And at any time, the Germans could just march in to your place and take whatever they wanted to or take away people that they thought looked like they were subversive or something. So we really didn't know from one moment to another what was going to happen to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember witnessing Germans taking people away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, in the street. And I also remember a couple of Germans coming into our home and admiring some of the beautiful things that we still had from way back. But whether they actually took something or not, I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember being scared of the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Well, there was a curfew. And there wasn't much food.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the curfew?

speaker: interviewee
text: The curfew-- we weren't allowed out after a certain time in the street. So every evening-- people had to do something in the evening-- so people in our-- in our flat used to get together and play cards, or talk, or have little soirees, a bit of music. They just had to somehow entertain each other, because otherwise, you could just go crazy thinking of what would happen. At some stage in the ghetto, rumors started that some of the other ghettos in Poland were being liquidated and that people were being taken to concentration camps and what the concentration camps were like. And I'm not sure whether there were rumors of ovens in, in-- when we were in the ghetto, but there was a fear that this was only a temporary measure, that the Germans really had plans to annihilate all the Jewish population of Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have newspapers or radios in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Could be. Probably, but I am not-- there probably would have been like a Jewish paper printed in the ghetto, but I really can't remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who went with you to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my parents, my aunt and my uncle and my cousin, and my grandmother. We all went as a group. And I think we, we sort of-- we ate together, and we shared a room. I think, at one stage, I shared a bed with my grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get food in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think people-- like probably they were baking bread and-- well, I really don't remember. Maybe vegetables were grown, or they were brought in from the outside. But there wasn't much of anything. And we were in the lucky position because, as I said, my uncle worked as a doctor. My father worked as an accountant. But in families where there weren't too many professional people or tradespeople, things were not good. And there was a lot of bartering going around in the ghetto. There were some who were better off and some who were worse off. And people sold off their-- probably their jewelry and their belongings, their ornaments, their household things, so that they could survive. It was very difficult. Very difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll stop the tape there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
